representative_quote: I think my favourite of all the things so far has been the teen hub because I have made some great friends there […] I am still quite an anxious person, but I am not nearly as bad as I used to be, and I have like found supports and friends who support me and the people around me who support me as well. So that is like the biggest change I think, mentally I have become a bit better. Rainbow Club Participant

“One of the frustrations within mental health services … projects are set up with a start and end date … and then that’s sort of it afterwards. … Our goal is to give them the opportunity afterwards to keep doing it … it’s about having that kind of lifelong approach to it.”

Because of the support, recognition and encouragement provided by Rethink Ireland at a crucial early phase, the Hope & Courage Collective has flourished and grown in ways we didn't think possible. Niamh McDonald, Director of Advocacy and Community Engagement, Hope & Courage Collective

[...] I am a big believer in social capital and I think if you put good pillars around a young person, you know, to explore their social network and if a person has a good social network, I mean, the benefits of that in terms of mental health – you are more connected, you are more involved, it builds your self-esteem, you have people you can go to for support.

The main challenge is sustaining engagement and ownership from players in their mental health journey. While awareness is rising, consistent help-seeking behaviours and embedding wellbeing as a core part of development remain uneven. Additionally, resources are finite so we need to balance the breadth of issues (acute mental health needs, transition, brain health, neurodiversity, everyday wellbeing) with the depth of support we can realistically provide.

I guess beforehand it was social anxiety I guess, like I was terrified of speaking with people just from issues I have had with people in school and stuff, so I was just super quiet as in talking to people. But it really brought me out of my shell. And especially being over with all the staff over there, it really helped build my confidence. Rainbow Club Youth Participant
“One simple model is a model like the No Barriers project … partnerships with gym settings and … other community organizations that help people get into physical activity and positive lifestyles.”

I believe the main challenge is poverty and its knock on effects to our students.
We have students who are hungry and unhygienic. We have many families battling addiction and anti social behaviour in their homes and surrounding areas. The trauma of growing up in this area impacts the majority of our students in many different ways from neglect to witnessing crime up to and including murder.
